verdict_reason: The engineering layer renders brāhmaṇasya vijānataḥ as 'practitioner who has reached the principle', which is a tradition-neutral rendering acceptable across Advaita (jñāna-niṣṭha brahmana), Vishishtadvaita (one whose ritual ends are subsumed within devotion to the Supreme), and Gaudiya Vaishnava (one who knows the purpose, viz. Krishna) readings. The layer does NOT attribute the verse to a single tradition's reading; it stays at the level of structural analogy (small-well : flood :: rule : principle), where all four traditions agree. The technical terms brāhmaṇa, vijānataḥ, and veda are dropped, but they are dropped to the same level the source pack itself drops them when distinguishing the realized/unrealized — i.e., the engineering analog rests on the structural distinction, not on technical Sanskrit lexicon. This is acceptable because (a) the verse's lexical content is preserved verbatim alongside the analog in the verse package, and (b) the analog does not import sectarian commitments that the dropped terms would carry. The 'first-principles judgment' framing is consistent with both Advaita and Vishishtadvaita readings; it does not flatten one into the other. Pass — but I note the analog is closer to Ramanuja's inclusivist framing ('rule operating inside the code') than Shankara's redundancy framing, without naming Ramanuja. This is a soft preference, not a conflation.
